title: Sandbox, and Out into the Real World
lesson:
You can read the LC-3 fluently now. That's the win. The LC-3 was designed by Patt and Patel for exactly this moment: small enough to fit on one page of the textbook, regular enough that once you have it, you have the shape of every modern RISC.

But the LC-3 itself doesn't exist. You can't buy a chip; it lives in a textbook. That's a deliberate teaching choice, not a limitation. The real test is whether what you learned transfers to actual silicon. Spoiler: it does.

Next stop is the most-used computer architecture in history, and you're almost certainly holding one right now. ARM is in every iPhone since 2007, every Android, every modern Apple Silicon Mac, and an increasing share of the racks at TACC. The patterns you just used on the LC-3 (load-store, condition codes, link-register subroutines, array walks with LDR) are the patterns you'll see when you read the assembly your iPhone actually runs. The names change. The ideas don't.
prompt: Have a play. The whole LC-3 is yours. Print messages with PUTS, build loops and subroutines (JSR and RET), walk arrays with LDR. Then travel onward to the phone in your pocket.
starterCode:
; Sandbox: no goal here. The LC-3 is yours to explore.
; This prints a message using a subroutine (JSR and RET).
.ORIG x3000
        LEA R0, MSG
        JSR PRINT      ; call the print routine
        HALT

; PRINT: prints the string whose address is in R0
PRINT   PUTS
        RET            ; return to the caller (uses R7)

MSG     .STRINGZ "HOOK EM"
.END
